mcqs.question: Low-level formatting of a disk involves:

options.text: Creating file system structures like superblock and root directory
Dividing the disk surface into sectors with header, data, and ECC fields
Partitioning the disk into logical drives
Installing the operating system on the disk

mcqs.explanation: Low-level (physical) formatting divides the blank disk into sectors, each containing a header, data area, and ECC (Error-Correcting Code). This is done at the factory.

mcqs.question: The Master Boot Record (MBR) is located at:

options.text: The last sector of the disk
The first sector (sector 0) of the boot disk
The first sector of each partition
A special reserved area in RAM

mcqs.explanation: The MBR occupies sector 0 (the very first sector) of the boot disk. It contains the bootstrap code, partition table, and a magic number (0x55AA).

mcqs.question: In sector sparing, when a bad sector is detected, the disk controller:

options.text: Marks the sector in the FAT and skips it
Transparently remaps the bad sector to a spare sector from a reserved pool
Halts the system and notifies the user
Reformats the entire track containing the bad sector

mcqs.explanation: Sector sparing (sector forwarding) involves the disk controller maintaining a map of bad sectors and transparently redirecting access to spare sectors. The OS is unaware of the remapping.

mcqs.question: Which swap-space location offers better performance?

options.text: Swap file within an existing file system
Raw partition dedicated to swap
Both perform identically
Network-based swap

mcqs.explanation: A raw partition dedicated to swap is faster because the OS manages it directly without file system overhead (no directory lookups, no file metadata). However, it is harder to resize.

mcqs.question: What is the total size of the Master Boot Record?

options.text: 1024 bytes
256 bytes
512 bytes
4096 bytes

mcqs.explanation: The MBR is exactly 512 bytes: 446 bytes of bootstrap code + 64 bytes for the partition table (4 entries of 16 bytes each) + 2 bytes for the magic number (0x55AA).

mcqs.question: Sector slipping differs from sector sparing in that:

options.text: It uses ECC to correct errors in place
It shifts adjacent sectors by one to fill the bad sector gap, preserving sequential order
It reformats the entire disk
It is only used for SSDs, not HDDs

mcqs.explanation: Sector slipping shifts all sectors after the bad one by one position. This preserves sequential access order, unlike sector sparing which redirects to a potentially distant spare area.

mcqs.question: In modern systems, swap space is managed at the granularity of:

options.text: Entire processes
Individual pages
Disk sectors
File system blocks

mcqs.explanation: Modern systems swap individual pages (typically 4 KB) rather than entire processes. The swap space is divided into page-sized slots, and a swap map tracks which slots are in use.

mcqs.question: The ECC field in a disk sector is used for:

options.text: Encrypting the data stored in the sector
Storing the file name associated with the sector
Detecting and correcting data errors during read operations
Managing the mapping between logical and physical sectors

mcqs.explanation: ECC (Error-Correcting Code) is computed when data is written and verified when data is read. It can detect and correct small errors (soft errors) caused by dust, vibration, or media degradation.
